steps.title: Tie the example to the claim
steps.content: The passage begins with Hoi An as an example of a place with high pedestrian traffic, then immediately warns that copying one walkability feature, such as human-scaled architecture, may not cause more walking in other cities. Ramirez's argument explains why that kind of simple replication may fail: researchers do not yet have a complete understanding of how people decide whether to walk, because those decisions depend on local norms, demographic characteristics, conditions, and needs.

steps.title: Reject narrower purposes
steps.content: A overemphasizes architecture, which is only one example of a feature someone might try to copy from Hoi An. B shifts the focus to comparing pedestrian traffic between cities, but the passage is concerned with whether walking can be increased elsewhere. C says the text presents a way to improve individual decision-making, while the passage only describes why that decision-making is complex and hard to predict. The main purpose is to discuss why finding a reliable method for increasing walking in any given city is difficult. The answer is D.
